preamble.summary: Psalm 94 is a communal lament calling on God as the 'God of vengeance' to rise up and judge the arrogant oppressors who crush the vulnerable — widows, orphans, immigrants — while claiming that God does not see. The psalm moves from urgent appeal (vv. 1-7) to wisdom instruction warning the fools to reconsider (vv. 8-11), to a beatitude for the one God disciplines (vv. 12-15), to personal testimony of God's sustaining help (vv. 16-23).
preamble.remarkable: The psalm opens with one of the most startling divine titles in the Psalter: El neqamot YHWH ('God of vengeance, O LORD'). The modern ear flinches at 'vengeance,' but the Hebrew concept of neqamah is closer to 'vindication' — it is the restoration of justice when the powerful have exploited the weak. The oppressors in this psalm are not foreign enemies but domestic tyrants who murder widows, kill orphans, and slaughter immigrants (v. 6) while smugly assuming God is oblivious (v. 7). The psalmist's cry for vengeance is not personal vindictiveness but a demand that the moral order be enforced. The psalm then pivots remarkably to the language of discipline and instruction (vv. 12-13), suggesting that suffering under oppression can itself be a form of divine education — though the oppressors remain culpable.
preamble.friction: The word neqamah ('vengeance') requires careful handling. In English, 'vengeance' implies personal spite or retaliatory rage. In Hebrew, neqamah is a legal concept — it is the authority's obligation to punish wrongdoing and restore the wronged. When the psalm calls God El neqamot, it is invoking God's role as the supreme judge who must act when human courts have failed. We retain 'vengeance' because 'vindication' is too soft for the raw emotion of the psalm's opening, but the reader should understand it as judicial action, not emotional rage.
preamble.connections: The catalogue of victims in verse 6 — widow, immigrant, orphan — echoes the covenant protections of Exodus 22:21-24 and Deuteronomy 10:18, where God specifically promises to defend these three groups. The oppressors' claim that 'the LORD does not see' (v. 7) echoes the arrogance condemned in Psalm 10:11 and Ezekiel 8:12. The beatitude in verse 12 ('blessed is the one you discipline') connects to the wisdom tradition of Proverbs 3:11-12 and anticipates the theology of Hebrews 12:5-11. The LXX assigns this psalm to David for Wednesday (the fourth day), placing it in the weekly liturgical rotation of the Second Temple.

verses.text_hebrew: אֵל־נְ֭קָמוֹת יְהוָ֑ה אֵ֖ל נְקָמ֣וֹת הוֹפִֽיַע׃
verses.text_kjv: O LORD God, to whom vengeance belongeth; O God, to whom vengeance belongeth, shew thyself.
verses.rendering:
O God of vengeance, LORD —
O God of vengeance, shine forth!
verses.expanded_rendering: The double invocation El neqamot YHWH, El neqamot hofia ('God of vengeance, O LORD, God of vengeance, shine forth!') is an urgent, almost desperate cry. The repetition signals intensity — this is not polite petition but raw demand. The verb hofia ('shine forth, appear, show yourself') is a theophany verb — it describes God's visible, powerful manifestation. It appears in Deuteronomy 33:2 for God's appearance at Sinai and in Psalm 80:2 as a plea for God to show his face. The psalmist is calling for God to appear as judge, to make himself visible in a world where injustice has been taken as evidence of his absence.
translator_notes: The title El neqamot ('God of vengeance') uses the plural neqamot, which is likely an intensive plural — not 'vengeances' but 'great vengeance' or 'comprehensive vindication.' God's vengeance is not one act but a thoroughgoing restoration of justice.

key_terms.note: neqamah from the root n-q-m describes the act of imposing consequences for wrongdoing. In the Hebrew Bible it is primarily a divine prerogative (Deuteronomy 32:35, 'Vengeance is mine'). It is not emotionally driven retaliation but the sovereign's obligation to enforce justice. When human courts fail, the oppressed appeal to God's neqamah as their last recourse.

verses.text_hebrew: הִ֭נָּשֵׂא שֹׁפֵ֣ט הָאָ֑רֶץ הָשֵׁ֥ב גְּ֝מ֗וּל עַל־גֵּאִֽים׃
verses.text_kjv: Lift up thyself, thou judge of the earth: render a reward to the proud.
verses.rendering:
Rise up, O judge of the earth;
repay the arrogant what they deserve.
translator_notes: The title shofet ha-arets ('judge of the earth') invokes God's role as the universal magistrate — not merely Israel's God but the judge of all the earth (as Abraham said in Genesis 18:25). The verb hashev ('repay, return') with gemul ('recompense, what is deserved') asks God to give the arrogant the consequences their actions have earned. The ge'im ('the arrogant, the proud') are those who believe themselves above accountability.

verses.text_hebrew: עַד־מָתַ֖י רְשָׁעִ֥ים ׀ יְהוָ֑ה עַד־מָ֝תַ֗י רְשָׁעִ֥ים יַעֲלֹֽזוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: LORD, how long shall the wicked, how long shall the wicked triumph?
verses.rendering:
How long will the wicked, O LORD —
how long will the wicked celebrate?
translator_notes: The ad matai ('how long?') repeated twice is the classic lament formula — it appears in Psalms 6:3, 13:1-2, 74:10, and 79:5. The verb ya'alozu ('they celebrate, they exult') describes the wicked's jubilant self-confidence. The broken syntax — 'how long will the wicked, O LORD — how long will the wicked celebrate?' — mimics the speaker's emotional state: the sentence can barely contain itself.

verses.text_hebrew: יַבִּ֣יעוּ יְדַבְּר֣וּ עָתָ֑ק יִֽ֝תְאַמְּר֗וּ כׇּל־פֹּ֥עֲלֵי אָֽוֶן׃
verses.text_kjv: How long shall they utter and speak hard things? and all the workers of iniquity boast themselves?
verses.rendering:
They pour out arrogant speech;
all the evildoers boast.
translator_notes: The verb yabbi'u ('they pour out, they gush') suggests words flowing without restraint — verbal incontinence. The word ataq ('arrogance, hardness, insolence') describes speech that is bold, presumptuous, and heedless. The verb yit'ammru ('they boast, they say great things about themselves') completes the picture: the wicked speak freely, boast openly, and face no consequence. The psalmist sees a world where evil speech goes unchecked.

verses.text_hebrew: עַמְּךָ֣ יְהוָ֣ה יְדַכְּא֑וּ וְ֝נַחֲלָתְךָ֗ יְעַנּֽוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: They break in pieces thy people, O LORD, and afflict thine heritage.
verses.rendering:
They crush your people, O LORD,
and oppress your inheritance.
translator_notes: The verbs yedakke'u ('they crush') and ye'annu ('they oppress, they afflict') describe sustained, systematic abuse. The victims are identified as ammekha ('your people') and nachalatekha ('your inheritance') — these are not random victims but people who belong to God. The language implies that attacking God's people is an assault on God's own property. The psalmist is building the case for divine intervention: this is not someone else's problem; it is yours, LORD.

verses.text_hebrew: אַלְמָנָ֣ה וְגֵ֣ר יַהֲרֹ֑גוּ וִ֖יתוֹמִ֣ים יְרַצֵּֽחוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: They slay the widow and the stranger, and murder the fatherless.
verses.rendering:
They kill the widow and the immigrant,
and they murder the fatherless.
verses.expanded_rendering: The three victims — almanah ('widow'), ger ('immigrant, resident alien'), and yetomim ('fatherless, orphans') — are the most vulnerable members of Israelite society. They have no male protector, no clan support, no legal advocate. The Torah specifically mandates their protection (Exodus 22:21-24, Deuteronomy 24:17-21) and threatens divine wrath against those who exploit them. Two different verbs for killing are used: yaharogu ('they slay') and yeratssechu ('they murder'). The first is a general term for killing; the second (from ratsach, the verb of the sixth commandment, 'you shall not murder') specifically denotes unlawful, culpable killing. The psalmist is charging the oppressors with violating the most fundamental commandments while targeting the people God has specifically sworn to defend.
translator_notes: We render ger as 'immigrant' rather than 'stranger' because the ger in Israelite law was a non-Israelite living permanently within Israel's borders — a resident alien with legal protections but social vulnerability. 'Stranger' in modern English is too vague; 'immigrant' captures the social reality more accurately.

key_terms.semantic_range: resident alien, sojourner, immigrant, foreigner living among Israelites
key_terms.note: The ger was a non-Israelite who had taken up permanent residence within Israel's territory. Unlike the nokhri (temporary foreigner), the ger was embedded in the community and entitled to legal protection. God repeatedly identifies with the ger: 'You shall love the ger, for you were gerim in the land of Egypt' (Deuteronomy 10:19). Oppressing the ger is a direct violation of covenant law.

verses.text_hebrew: וַ֭יֹּאמְרוּ לֹ֣א יִרְאֶה־יָּ֑הּ וְלֹא־יָ֝בִ֗ין אֱלֹהֵ֥י יַעֲקֹֽב׃
verses.text_kjv: Yet they say, The LORD shall not see, neither shall the God of Jacob regard it.
verses.rendering:
And they say, "The LORD does not see;
the God of Jacob pays no attention."
translator_notes: The oppressors' theology is stated explicitly: lo yir'eh Yah ('the LORD does not see') and lo yavin Elohei Ya'aqov ('the God of Jacob does not understand'). This is practical atheism — not denying God's existence but denying his attentiveness. They believe God is too distant, too disinterested, or too powerless to notice their crimes. The psalmist will demolish this claim in the next four verses with devastating logic.

verses.text_hebrew: בִּ֭ינוּ בֹּעֲרִ֣ים בָּעָ֑ם וּ֝כְסִילִ֗ים מָתַ֥י תַּשְׂכִּֽילוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: Understand, ye brutish among the people: and ye fools, when will ye be wise?
verses.rendering:
Pay attention, you senseless ones among the people!
You fools — when will you understand?
translator_notes: The psalm shifts to wisdom instruction. The bo'arim ('senseless ones, brutish ones') and kesilim ('fools') are the same terms used in Psalm 92:7. The verb binu ('understand, pay attention') and taskilu ('will you have insight') are wisdom vocabulary — the psalmist is playing the role of teacher addressing willfully ignorant students. The question matai taskilu ('when will you understand?') implies the answer should be obvious.

verses.text_hebrew: הֲנֹ֣טַע אֹ֭זֶן הֲלֹ֣א יִשְׁמָ֑ע אִֽם־יֹ֥צֵֽר עַ֝֗יִן הֲלֹ֣א יַבִּֽיט׃
verses.text_kjv: He that planted the ear, shall he not hear? he that formed the eye, shall he not see?
verses.rendering:
He who planted the ear — does he not hear?
He who formed the eye — does he not see?
verses.expanded_rendering: The argument is devastatingly simple: the one who designed hearing cannot be deaf; the one who engineered sight cannot be blind. The verb nota ('planted') describes God embedding the ear in the human head like planting a seed in soil. The verb yotser ('formed, fashioned') is the potter's verb — God shaped the eye like an artisan forming clay. The logic is inescapable: the creator of sensory organs possesses the capacities those organs provide. If you can hear, it is because God who made your ear can hear infinitely more. If you can see, it is because God who shaped your eye sees everything. The oppressors' claim that God 'does not see' (v. 7) is not merely wrong — it is absurd.
translator_notes: This is one of the most elegant arguments in the Hebrew Bible. It does not appeal to revelation or authority but to common sense: the maker must possess what the made thing possesses. The form is rhetorical question — the expected answer is an emphatic 'yes.'

verses.text_hebrew: הֲיֹסֵ֣ר גּ֭וֹיִם הֲלֹ֣א יוֹכִ֑יחַ הַֽמְלַמֵּ֖ד אָדָ֣ם דָּֽעַת׃
verses.text_kjv: He that chastiseth the heathen, shall not he correct? he that teacheth man knowledge, shall not he know?
verses.rendering:
He who disciplines nations — will he not correct?
He who teaches humanity knowledge —
translator_notes: The argument extends from the senses (v. 9) to governance and instruction. The verb yoser ('disciplines') and yokiach ('corrects, convicts') are terms for judicial and parental authority. The one who holds nations accountable will certainly hold individuals accountable. The final phrase hamelammed adam da'at ('he who teaches humanity knowledge') is left without a predicate in the Hebrew — the sentence breaks off, as if the conclusion is so obvious it need not be stated. Some translations supply 'shall he not know?' but the Hebrew lets the silence speak.

verses.text_hebrew: יְֽהוָ֗ה יֹ֭דֵעַ מַחְשְׁב֣וֹת אָדָ֑ם כִּי־הֵ֥מָּה הָֽבֶל׃
verses.text_kjv: The LORD knoweth the thoughts of man, that they are vanity.
verses.rendering:
The LORD knows the thoughts of humanity —
they are a breath.
translator_notes: The conclusion: God does know — he knows human machshevot ('thoughts, plans, designs') and he judges them as hevel ('vapor, breath, vanity'). The word hevel is the key term of Ecclesiastes ('vanity of vanities'), meaning something insubstantial, transient, and ultimately without weight. The oppressors' plans, which seem so solid and successful, are mere vapor in God's assessment. Paul quotes this verse in 1 Corinthians 3:20.

verses.text_hebrew: אַ֥שְׁרֵי הַגֶּ֗בֶר אֲשֶׁר־תְּיַסְּרֶ֥נּוּ יָּ֑הּ וּ֝מִתּוֹרָתְךָ֗ תְלַמְּדֶֽנּוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: Blessed is the man whom thou chastenest, O LORD, and teachest him out of thy law;
verses.rendering:
How blessed is the one you discipline, O LORD,
the one you teach from your instruction —
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalm pivots dramatically from denouncing the wicked to pronouncing a blessing on the one God disciplines. The word ashrei ('blessed, happy, fortunate') opens the beatitude. The verb teyassrennu ('you discipline him') uses yasar, which means both 'to instruct' and 'to correct through suffering.' Combined with umittoratekha telammennu ('and from your torah you teach him'), the verse presents suffering under oppression as a form of divine education. God's torah ('instruction, teaching, law') is the curriculum; discipline is the pedagogy. This is not a justification of suffering but a reframing: even when the wicked appear to triumph, the righteous person is being shaped by the experience.
translator_notes: The word torah here means 'instruction' in its broadest sense — not merely the written law but God's ongoing teaching through life experience. The pairing of discipline (yasar) with teaching (lamad) insists that God's corrective action is always educational, never merely punitive.

key_terms.semantic_range: blessed, happy, fortunate, to be envied
key_terms.note: ashrei is a plural construct meaning something like 'the happinesses of' — it describes a state of well-being that comes from alignment with God's purposes. It opens Psalm 1 and appears throughout the Psalter as a marker of the good life. Here it is applied surprisingly to the one being disciplined, not the one at ease.

verses.text_hebrew: לְהַשְׁקִ֣יט ל֭וֹ מִימֵ֣י רָ֑ע עַ֤ד יִכָּרֶ֖ה לָרָשָׁ֣ע שָֽׁחַת׃
verses.text_kjv: That thou mayest give him rest from the days of adversity, until the pit be digged for the wicked.
verses.rendering:
giving him rest from days of trouble,
until a pit is dug for the wicked.
translator_notes: The purpose of discipline is lehashqit ('to give rest, to grant quiet') — the disciplined one receives inner calm during the mi-yemei ra ('days of evil, days of trouble'). This rest is not removal from suffering but composure within it. Meanwhile, the wicked are heading toward a shachat ('pit') being dug for them. The image is of a hunter's pit-trap: the wicked walk confidently forward, unaware that the ground ahead has been excavated.

verses.text_hebrew: כִּ֤י לֹא־יִטֹּ֣שׁ יְהוָ֣ה עַמּ֑וֹ וְ֝נַחֲלָת֗וֹ לֹ֣א יַעֲזֹֽב׃
verses.text_kjv: For the LORD will not cast off his people, neither will he forsake his inheritance.
verses.rendering:
For the LORD will not abandon his people;
he will not forsake his inheritance.
translator_notes: Two verbs of rejection — yittosh ('will cast off, will abandon') and ya'azov ('will forsake') — are both negated. God's commitment to ammo ('his people') and nachalato ('his inheritance') is permanent. The same assurance appears in 1 Samuel 12:22 and Romans 11:1-2. The verse grounds the believer's endurance: the discipline of verses 12-13 is not abandonment but pedagogy.

verses.text_hebrew: כִּֽי־עַד־צֶ֭דֶק יָשׁ֣וּב מִשְׁפָּ֑ט וְ֝אַחֲרָ֗יו כׇּל־יִשְׁרֵי־לֵֽב׃
verses.text_kjv: But judgment shall return unto righteousness: and all the upright in heart shall follow it.
verses.rendering:
For justice will return to righteousness,
and all the upright in heart will follow it.
translator_notes: The verse promises that mishpat ('justice, judgment') will return to tsedeq ('righteousness'). Justice has strayed — the wicked have corrupted the courts, perverted the legal system. But it will come back to its proper alignment with righteousness. When it does, kol yishrei lev ('all the upright in heart') will follow — the righteous community will rally to justice restored. The verse envisions a future realignment of the moral order.

verses.text_hebrew: מִֽי־יָק֣וּם לִ֭י עִם־מְרֵעִ֑ים מִֽי־יִתְיַצֵּ֥ב לִ֝֗י עִם־פֹּ֥עֲלֵי אָֽוֶן׃
verses.text_kjv: Who will rise up for me against the evildoers? or who will stand up for me against the workers of iniquity?
verses.rendering:
Who will rise up for me against the evildoers?
Who will take a stand for me against the workers of wickedness?
translator_notes: The psalmist now speaks personally. The questions mi yaqum li ('who will rise for me?') and mi yityatsev li ('who will stand for me?') express the isolation of the righteous person facing organized evil alone. The implied answer is: no human ally appeared — only God (as the next verse reveals).

verses.text_hebrew: לוּלֵ֣י יְ֭הוָה עֶזְרָ֣תָה לִּ֑י כִּ֝מְעַ֗ט שָׁכְנָ֥ה דוּמָ֗ה נַפְשִֽׁי׃
verses.text_kjv: Unless the LORD had been my help, my soul had almost dwelt in silence.
verses.rendering:
If the LORD had not been my help,
my soul would soon have dwelt in silence.
translator_notes: The word dumah ('silence') is a euphemism for death — the silence of the grave, where no praise is offered (Psalm 115:17). The phrase kim'at ('almost, soon, nearly') reveals how close the psalmist came to destruction. The counterfactual construction (lulei, 'if not') attributes survival entirely to God: without divine intervention, death was imminent.

verses.text_hebrew: אִם־אָ֭מַרְתִּי מָ֣טָה רַגְלִ֑י חַסְדְּךָ֥ יְ֝הוָ֗ה יִסְעָדֵֽנִי׃
verses.text_kjv: When I said, My foot slippeth; thy mercy, O LORD, held me up.
verses.rendering:
When I said, "My foot is slipping,"
your faithful love, O LORD, held me up.
translator_notes: The image of the slipping foot (matah ragli) represents moral or existential collapse — losing one's footing in life. At exactly that moment, chasdekha YHWH ('your faithful love, O LORD') intervened. The verb yis'adeni ('it supported me, it held me up') is from sa'ad, which means to sustain, to prop up, to brace. God's chesed is not a feeling but a structural support that prevents collapse.

verses.text_hebrew: בְּרֹ֣ב שַׂרְעַפַּ֣י בְּקִרְבִּ֑י תַּ֝נְחוּמֶ֗יךָ יְשַׁעַשְׁע֥וּ נַפְשִֽׁי׃
verses.text_kjv: In the multitude of my thoughts within me thy comforts delight my soul.
verses.rendering:
When anxious thoughts multiply within me,
your consolations delight my soul.
translator_notes: The word sar'appai ('my anxious thoughts, my disquieting thoughts') is a rare word suggesting mental turmoil — thoughts that branch and multiply uncontrollably. The be-rov ('in the multitude, when they are many') intensifies the picture. Against this flood of anxiety, God provides tanchumekha ('your consolations, your comforts'), and these consolations yesha'ash'u nafshi ('delight my soul'). The verb sha'asha ('to delight, to fondle, to caress') is a term of deep pleasure. The psalm honestly describes anxiety and honestly describes its remedy: divine comfort that produces actual delight, not mere endurance.

verses.text_hebrew: הַֽ֭יְחׇבְרְךָ כִּסֵּ֣א הַוּ֑וֹת יֹצֵ֖ר עָמָ֣ל עֲלֵי־חֹֽק׃
verses.text_kjv: Shall the throne of iniquity have fellowship with thee, which frameth mischief by a law?
verses.rendering:
Can a corrupt throne be allied with you —
one that creates oppression under cover of law?
translator_notes: The kisse havvot ('throne of destruction/corruption') refers to a seat of power that legislates injustice. The phrase yotser amal alei choq ('fashioning oppression by decree') is one of the most politically charged statements in the Psalter: it describes rulers who use legal systems to institutionalize suffering. The rhetorical question expects the answer 'No' — God cannot be allied with rulers who weaponize law against the vulnerable. This verse condemns systemic, legalized injustice as incompatible with God's character.

verses.text_hebrew: יָ֭גוֹדּוּ עַל־נֶ֣פֶשׁ צַדִּ֑יק וְדָ֖ם נָקִ֣י יַרְשִֽׁיעוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: They gather themselves together against the soul of the righteous, and condemn the innocent blood.
verses.rendering:
They band together against the life of the righteous
and condemn innocent blood.
translator_notes: The verb yagoddu ('they band together, they attack in a mob') describes coordinated group violence against the nefesh tsaddiq ('the life/soul of the righteous'). The phrase dam naqi yarshi'u ('they condemn innocent blood') refers to judicial murder — using the legal system to execute the guiltless. The combination of mob action and legal machinery creates a picture of total injustice: both the streets and the courts have been captured by the wicked.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיְהִ֤י יְהוָ֣ה לִ֭י לְמִשְׂגָּ֑ב וֵ֝אלֹהַ֗י לְצ֣וּר מַחְסִֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: But the LORD is my defence; and my God is the rock of my refuge.
verses.rendering:
But the LORD has been my fortress,
and my God the rock of my refuge.
translator_notes: Against the assembled forces of institutional wickedness, the psalmist has one resource: YHWH li le-misgav ('the LORD is my fortress'). The misgav ('high fortress, stronghold') is an elevated defensive position — a cliff fortress beyond the reach of attackers. The tsur machasi ('rock of my refuge') combines the imagery of immovable stone with sheltering safety. The two metaphors — fortress and rock — together convey both elevation (beyond reach) and solidity (beyond destruction).

verses.text_hebrew: וַיָּ֤שֶׁב עֲלֵיהֶ֨ם ׀ אֶת־אוֹנָ֗ם וּבְרָעָתָ֥ם יַצְמִיתֵ֑ם יַ֝צְמִיתֵ֗ם יְהוָ֥ה אֱלֹהֵֽינוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: And he shall bring upon them their own iniquity, and shall cut them off in their own wickedness; yea, the LORD our God shall cut them off.
verses.rendering:
He will turn their own evil back upon them
and destroy them for their wickedness.
The LORD our God will destroy them.
translator_notes: The final verse returns to the vengeance theme of verse 1. The verb vayyashev ('he will turn back, he will repay') describes God redirecting the oppressors' onam ('their wickedness') back onto them — their own weapons become their undoing. The verb yatsmitem ('he will annihilate them') is repeated twice for emphasis — total, complete destruction. The psalm ends with the full covenantal name: YHWH Eloheinu ('the LORD our God'), claiming God as the community's own. The God who seemed absent (v. 7) has the last word.
